title: Marketing Consulting Services

narrative.executive_view: Marketing consulting has a real near-term AI labor dividend, particularly in research synthesis, routine content, reporting, proposals and coordination. It is nevertheless a difficult buyout thesis: client-side marketing teams are adopting the same tools, delivery savings are likely to be competed into renewals, and much of a small firm's revenue follows its principal. Buy only a specialized, retainer-heavy operator with transferable accounts and execution/data integration; otherwise this is a cautious, pressured-demand situation rather than a broad roll-up opportunity. [S1][S2][S4]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Generative AI can accelerate first drafts of positioning, personas, competitive scans, campaign analyses, briefs, presentations and status reporting. The CMO Survey reports generative AI deployed across 15.1% of marketing activities in 2025 and a 10.8% reported reduction in marketing overhead, useful evidence that the work is becoming tool-assisted rather than hypothetical. Client workshops, senior persuasion, accountable recommendations and relationship selling remain human-intensive, so the task judgment is partial rather than a claim that whole occupations disappear. [S1]
narrative.value_capture: The operator should retain only a minority of the gross labor saving. Project fees and retainers can initially preserve a spread when deliverables are produced faster, but buyers increasingly ask consulting firms to demonstrate AI delivery economics and clients can use the same tools internally. I model distribution/pass-through at 0.35 and moat retention at 0.30: vertical expertise, proprietary client data, martech implementation and sticky execution can protect selected firms, while a generalist strategy deck is readily benchmarked and portable with the partner. This is distinct from terminal demand: it measures who keeps a saving if work remains. [S1][S4]
narrative.adoption_timing: Current adoption is below the 50% boundary on the closest directly measured metric: the 2025 CMO Survey says generative AI is deployed across 15.1% of marketing activities and projects 44.2% of all marketing efforts using AI within three years. I therefore use 15.1% as a conservative, scope-mismatched proxy and estimate three years to 50% effective adoption in the target operators. That is an adoption estimate, not an inference from owner age or consolidation. [S1]
narrative.consolidation_economics: The supplied band has 1,936 estimated $1–10M EBITDA targets, but transaction comparables are imperfect. BizBuySell's full-year 2025 tables are voluntary broker-reported small-business transactions and use cash flow/SDE rather than normalized EBITDA; they show the broad small-business market is liquid enough to inform a low-end entry anchor, not an exact marketing-consulting multiple. Equiteq describes continuing selective consulting M&A but says buyers favor scale, specialization and defensibility. I use a 4.5x normalized EBITDA buy estimate and 5.0x exit estimate only for a qualifying specialist, with modest active-consolidator evidence rather than assuming broad multiple expansion. [S3][S4]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. Marketing leaders still need accountable outside perspective and execution capacity, but the product has no license or physical moat and clients can generate research, planning and creative drafts internally. The same evidence showing AI in marketing operations and buyer scrutiny of AI economics supports pressure on commoditized advisory hours; it does not demonstrate that all external marketing advice vanishes. Durable paid demand is most plausible where the firm owns client-specific data, vertical knowledge, media/martech execution or an embedded retainer. [S1][S4]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The role mix is a 4-digit 5416 proxy rather than this marketing-consulting code and the residual creative/content tail may be more exposed than the listed occupations. The adoption survey measures client marketing activity, not AI deployment by US lower-middle-market consultancies. Owner-readiness and transaction evidence are national, cross-industry samples; neither proves owner age, succession scarcity, client transferability or EBITDA multiples for this code. Finally, agency, advertising, market-research and martech benchmarks cross NAICS boundaries. These uncertainties justify MED overall confidence and disciplined deal-level diligence on renewals, client concentration, founder dependence and normalized compensation. [S1][S2][S3][S5]

sources.title: The CMO Survey: Marketers Claim a Broader Role and Increased Influence Amid Pressures
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 survey of 281 senior US marketing leaders: AI powers 17.2% of marketing efforts, generative AI is deployed across 15.1%, projected all-AI integration is 44.2% within three years, and leaders report lower marketing overhead.

sources.title: Salesforce State of Marketing, Ninth Edition
sources.what_it_supports: Vendor survey of nearly 5,000 marketers describing AI investment and implementation as a marketing priority and challenge; directional support only for broad practitioner adoption.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report Data Tables: Full-Year 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Broker-reported US closed small-business transaction tables, including sale price, revenue and cash-flow multiples; supports only a broad lower-end market-comparability anchor.

sources.title: Equiteq Specialty Consulting M&A Report – 2H 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Consulting M&A activity was more selective in H2 2025; buyers favored scaled, specialized, defensible firms and sought evidence of AI delivery economics.

sources.title: Exit Planning Institute 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report
sources.what_it_supports: National owner-readiness survey of over 1,162 owners: 75% would like to exit within ten years, 42% report a written formal business transition plan, and 73% of privately held companies are expected to transition within ten years.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541613 ($34,007,872,680) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541613 ($79,535,987,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $282,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541613); class <5: 41,556 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,884 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,727,383 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,925 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,595,461 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $562,690 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,662 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,903,738 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,393,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 274 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,364,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,412,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,739,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,895,655 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,936
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Task-level judgment: marketing AI evidence supports substantial assistance for drafting, analysis and routine coordination, while relationship, accountable judgment and live client work remain. The 4-digit role mix is only a proxy and the residual is explicitly assigned.
caveats: Source adoption is marketing-practitioner evidence, not task observation in NAICS 541613.
Supplied occupational weights are 4-digit 5416 and likely underrepresent creative and marketing-specialist roles.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research synthesis, analyses and deck drafts automate; executive judgment and workshops persist.
Reporting and internal coordination automate; principals retain client ownership and decisions.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Segmentation, research summaries and campaign analysis are highly tool-assisted; interpretation remains human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposals and pitch materials automate, while relationship selling and referrals persist.
Scheduling, tracking and status reporting automate; expectation management persists.
Routine responses and knowledge retrieval automate; escalations remain human.
Routine business analysis and documentation are exposed, with specialist exceptions.
Coding is AI-augmented but implementation, integration and QA retain human work.
Bookkeeping and close support automate substantially, subject to review.
HR administration and document work automate; employee matters require judgment.
Unlisted creative, content and digital roles are exposed to AI production, but include specialist and client-facing work.

pi_dist.rationale: Fixed-fee work can retain some productivity spread, but client-side tool access and buyer focus on delivery economics create material renewal and procurement pass-through.
caveats: No code-specific fixed-fee versus time-and-materials share was located.
AI use is a distinct cost-pass-through channel, not counted as task replaceability.

pi_moat.rationale: Specialization and demonstrable client value are buyer priorities, but generic marketing advice lacks a statutory or physical moat and client books can follow principals.
caveats: Equiteq covers specialty consulting broadly, not US lower-middle-market marketing consulting.
Deal-specific data ownership, retainer stickiness and key-person dependence can change this sharply.

t50_years.rationale: The closest current metric is below 50%, while surveyed leaders project 44.2% of marketing efforts using AI in three years; effective use in small consulting operators is estimated to reach 50% on a similar horizon.
caveats: The cited survey measures client marketing activity rather than target-operator adoption.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The CMO Survey directly reports generative AI deployed across 15.1% of marketing activities in its 2025 US senior-marketer sample.
current_adoption_pct.plausible_range: 0.10-0.25 as a proxy for target work
caveats: This is activity share at client marketing organizations, not firm adoption or NAICS 541613 share.

historical_analogs.value: Marketing technology adoption typically augments production before standardizing client expectations; this is directional context, not a quantified analogue.
historical_analogs.rationale: Observed rapid marketing AI use and buyer scrutiny support a fast diffusion/competitive-response analogy.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Rapid adoption with uncertain fee response
caveats: No verified code-specific historical analogue was used for a score.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A 32% over-60 estimate is a conservative cross-industry bridge from the national owner-transition evidence; it is not directly observed for this NAICS.
caveats: EPI's report does not publish a NAICS 541613 or exact 60-plus cut in the support used.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: EPI reports 75% of surveyed owners would like to exit within ten years while 42% report a written formal business transition plan, documenting a national readiness gap.
caveats: This is national, cross-industry owner-readiness evidence and does not establish a shortage specific to marketing consulting.

active_consolidators.rationale: A small positive count represents active strategic and sponsor buyer categories inferred from continuing specialty-consulting M&A, not a measured count of marketing-consulting consolidators.
caveats: Equiteq provides market direction but no count for this NAICS or EBITDA band.

buy_mult.rationale: A normalized 4.5x EBITDA entry estimate sits above broad broker-reported SDE/cash-flow data for micro businesses but below premium specialty-consulting expectations, contingent on transferable clients.
caveats: BizBuySell reports cash flow/SDE, not EBITDA, and is not NAICS-specific.
SDE must be normalized for owner compensation before comparison.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest 0.5x expansion assumes the acquired firm proves specialization, retained accounts and AI-enabled delivery; it does not assume broad market multiple expansion.
caveats: Specialty-consulting market evidence is not a direct lower-middle-market marketing-consulting transaction comp.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed project fees and retainers, often scoped from labor estimates; AI productivity creates a direct renewal/procurement pass-through risk.
pricing_structure.rationale: This reflects the industry business model and the cited evidence of client marketing AI use and buyer focus on delivery economics.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: More fixed-fee/retainer work improves temporary retention; hourly work raises pass-through.
caveats: No verified NAICS-specific pricing-model census was found.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. A substantial task-level estimate is paired with a positive three-year t50 because the direct proxy is only 15.1% deployed generative AI activity, below the 50% boundary.

terminal_value.justification: Outside perspective and execution can survive, but internal AI enables clients to substitute for commoditized research, planning and creative work; durable demand requires specialization and embedded execution.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. Role mix is 4-digit 5416; adoption is a senior-marketer activity survey; ownership and transaction sources are national/cross-industry; consulting M&A evidence is specialty-consulting rather than this NAICS.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Included only in pi_dist: faster delivery can be passed through through renewal, procurement or client insourcing. It is not counted as a human floor, terminal-demand protection or task replaceability.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 4-digit 5416 occupation mix, 2024/2022 labor-share vintage and estimated band population are deterministic inputs but imperfectly map to marketing consulting.
sensitivity: A higher creative/content residual exposure raises V.
Different normalized owner compensation or margin changes the true $1-10M target count and comparability.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Marketing strategy consulting with adjacent execution work
market_boundary.selection_basis: NAICS title and prompt scope; external agency and consulting evidence is disclosed as adjacent rather than treated as an exact mapping.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 5.0 to 2.0 across the stated range.
Fee pass-through materially changes C and whether labor savings accrue to the operator.
Client transferability and specialization dominate retained savings and terminal value.
Changes V through the deterministic labor share.

reviewer_note: Research supports a selective rather than broad acquisition thesis. Verify client concentration, retainer renewal language, founder transition, normalized owner compensation and whether the target owns data/execution before relying on the score.
